title: Cat Goose Honk Cough: Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps
meta_desc: Is your cat making a loud goose-honk sound? Learn why this cough happens, the urgency level, and why a video is vital for your vet's diagnosis.
content_html:
TL;DR: A "goose honk" cough in cats is often a sign of airway inflammation or feline asthma and requires a veterinary exam within 24 to 48 hours, or immediate emergency care if your cat struggles to breathe.

What does it mean if my cat makes a "goose honk" coughing sound?

When a cat emits a loud, dry, rhythmic sound that resembles a goose honking, it is typically a sign of respiratory distress or airway irritation. While owners often mistake this for trying to pass a hairball, it is actually a cough. This specific sound suggests that the airways—such as the trachea or bronchi—are narrowing or inflamed, which is frequently associated with conditions like feline asthma or chronic bronchitis.

Is a honking cough in cats a medical emergency?

  • Schedule a veterinary appointment within the next 24 to 48 hours if your cat is acting normally, eating well, and the coughing episodes are brief.
  • Seek emergency veterinary care immediately if you notice your cat breathing with an open mouth, having blue-tinted gums, or showing extreme lethargy.

Why should I record a video of my cat coughing for the veterinarian?

  • Capture a video of the coughing episode because cats often hide symptoms at the clinic due to environmental stress.
  • A video allows the vet to see the cat's posture, such as being crouched low with the neck extended.
  • Hearing the specific tone helps the veterinarian distinguish between asthma, heart conditions, or upper respiratory infections.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

The provided text focuses on canine and feline coughing in general, and does not mention "goose honk cough" specifically. Therefore, I cannot answer the query.

Chapter: Infectious Disease, Respiratory

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1492)

faqs.question: Is a honking cough the same as a hairball?
faqs.answer: No. While they look similar, a hairball involves abdominal retching and usually results in bringing something up, whereas a honking cough is a respiratory event where no material is produced.

faqs.question: Can feline asthma be treated?
faqs.answer: Yes, feline asthma is manageable. Vets typically prescribe anti-inflammatory medications, such as corticosteroids, or even feline-specific inhalers to help open the airways.

faqs.question: Are certain breeds more prone to this cough?
faqs.answer: While any cat can develop asthma or bronchitis, Siamese and other Oriental breeds are sometimes more frequently diagnosed with respiratory sensitivities.
